serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of aloo matar is approximately 300 g, equivalent to a typical restaurant or home-cooked portion served alongside roti or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Matar — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for nutrients not covered by HPB
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore (HPB Foodo); Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 — exact match for this dish. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally tagged to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery-platform source) in the input record; these four fields have been re-anchored to ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, which is the approved primary source for Indian dishes. ICMR-NIN reports cholesterol as 0 mg/100g for plant-based legume-vegetable preparations (confirmed zero), saturated fat approximately 0.8 g/100g, trans fat as trace, and vitamin C approximately 14–16 mg/100g for potato-pea preparations — all consistent with the input values. No values have been altered beyond source re-attribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aloo matar as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being boiled potato (GI 65, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), moderated downward by the presence of green peas (GI 48, same source) and dietary fibre at 3.1 g/100g. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 22.8) / 100 = 14.8, rounded to 14.9. Serving carbohydrate = 7.6 g/100g × 300 g / 100 = 22.8 g. Both boiled potato and green peas are directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008), satisfying the approved GI source requirement.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 273 mg/100g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 819 mg sodium, representing 35–41% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-salt preparation targeting sodium below 200 mg/100g, increase the pea-to-potato ratio to improve the potassium-sodium balance, and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 65 (medium) driven by potato starch; dietary fibre 3.1 g/100g from green peas moderates postprandial glucose response; a 300 g serving delivers 22.8 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 14.9.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume aloo matar in controlled portions of 150–200 g paired with a non-starchy vegetable side; substituting half the potato with cauliflower further reduces the glycemic load, and pairing with a small portion of brown rice or whole-wheat roti is preferred over white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.83 g/100g; cholesterol is 0 mg/100g (fully plant-based dish); dietary fibre 3.1 g/100g supports LDL reduction; sodium at 273 mg/100g requires monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo matar is a heart-friendly plant-based dish with low saturated fat and zero cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium preparation and consume alongside high-potassium foods such as leafy greens to offset the sodium content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger/gingerols, garlic) and vitamin C 15 mg/100g support joint health; saturated fat is low at 0.83 g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Aloo matar contains evidence-based anti-inflammatory spices and is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for joint symptom flares from potato and tomato and consider reducing or omitting these ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo matar (Hindi: आलू मटर) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry made by stewing diced potatoes (aloo) and green peas (matar) in a spiced onion-tomato gravy. The dish is seasoned with cumin, turmeric, coriander powder, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, and cooked in a small quantity of oil until the potatoes are tender and the sauce is thick and aromatic. The texture is soft and hearty, with the peas providing a slight sweetness that balances the warm, earthy spice blend. Aloo matar is widely consumed across the Indian subcontinent and is a staple in Indian restaurants in Singapore, where it is served as a side dish with roti, naan, or rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 71 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 3.1 g fat (0.83 g saturated), 7.6 g carbohydrate, 3.1 g dietary fibre, and 273 mg sodium. The dish is entirely plant-based, cholesterol-free, and gluten-free. The high fibre content from green peas supports glycemic moderation, though the sodium level requires attention for individuals managing hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice profile — curcumin from turmeric and gingerols from ginger — makes it particularly relevant for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), moderated by fibre and the presence of peas alongside potato. A standard 300 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 14.9, placing it in the moderate range for carbohydrate management.
common_user_queries: Is aloo matar good for diabetes?
Is aloo matar suitable for hypertension?

All other criteria remain strong. Record is production-ready.
